content.text: Why Security, Governance, and Manageability Are Cloud Benefits

In an on-premises data center, security certifications, governance frameworks, and management tooling must be built and maintained from scratch. A cloud provider like Azure invests billions of dollars annually in these areas and delivers them as services — your organization inherits and extends a security and compliance posture that would be prohibitively expensive to replicate independently.

For the AZ-900 exam, these three properties are listed as cloud benefits alongside reliability, scalability, and cost. Understanding what each means and what Azure service implements it is the core objective of this topic.

attrs.alt: Azure Security, Governance, and Manageability stack showing key services in each pillar and the Zero Trust principle spanning all three
attrs.title: Azure Security, Governance and Manageability Stack

content.text: Cloud security in Azure is built on the shared responsibility model: the provider handles security of the cloud (physical facilities, hypervisor, network fabric, global DDoS capacity), while the customer handles security in the cloud (OS configuration, application code, identity management, data classification).
As you move up the service stack (IaaS to PaaS to SaaS), Azure takes on more responsibility. Identity and data classification remain your responsibility regardless of the service model.

Traditional security assumed a trusted internal network and untrusted internet. Cloud dissolves that perimeter. Zero Trust replaces network trust with identity trust, applying to every access request regardless of origin.
Authenticate and authorize every request using identity, location, device compliance, service, workload, and data classification. Implemented via Conditional Access policies in Entra ID.
Grant only the minimum permissions needed for the minimum time needed. Implemented via Azure RBAC with just-in-time access through Privileged Identity Management (PIM).
Design as if the network is already compromised. Microsegment networks, encrypt all traffic end-to-end, and log everything to detect lateral movement.
Defender for Cloud provides continuous security assessment, Secure Score, threat detection, and regulatory compliance tracking across Azure, on-premises (via Arc), AWS, and GCP.

# Enable Defender for Cloud workload protection plans
az security pricing create --name VirtualMachines --tier Standard
az security pricing create --name SqlServers --tier Standard
az security pricing create --name AppServices --tier Standard

# View Secure Score (0-100, higher = better posture)
az security secure-score list \
  --query '[].{name:name,score:properties.score.current,max:properties.score.max}' -o table

# List active security recommendations
az security assessment list \
  --query "[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy'].{resource:properties.resourceDetails.id,rec:displayName}" \
  -o table
Microsoft Entra ID: Identity as the Security Perimeter
In cloud, identity is the new perimeter. Entra ID handles authentication and authorization for all Azure resources, Microsoft 365, and thousands of third-party SaaS applications.

# View existing Conditional Access policies
az ad conditional-access policy list \
  --query '[].{name:displayName,state:state}' -o table

# Privileged Identity Management (PIM): just-in-time role elevation
# Users are not permanently assigned privileged roles.
# They activate the role for a time-limited window (max 8h by default).
# View eligible role assignments
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilitySchedules?api-version=2020-10-01" \
  --query 'value[].{role:properties.roleDefinitionId,principal:properties.principalId,expiry:properties.endDateTime}'

Key Vault centralizes management of secrets, encryption keys, and TLS certificates. Applications retrieve secrets at runtime using managed identity — no credentials in code, environment variables, or config files.

# Retrieve a secret from Key Vault using Managed Identity (no credentials in code)
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

cred = DefaultAzureCredential()  # Uses managed identity when running in Azure
client = SecretClient(vault_url="https://my-vault.vault.azure.net", credential=cred)

# The application retrieves the DB password at runtime
# Key Vault logs every access: who retrieved what, when, from which IP
db_password = client.get_secret("db-password").value

# Certificates: Key Vault auto-renews TLS certificates from DigiCert/GlobalSign
# and delivers them directly to App Service and Application Gateway

Cloud governance is the framework of policies, role assignments, and resource organization that ensures Azure workloads comply with organizational standards and regulatory requirements automatically, at scale.
Containers for multiple subscriptions. Policies and RBAC applied at MG level are inherited by all subscriptions and resources below. Supports up to 6 levels of nesting.
Billing and trust boundary. Common pattern: one subscription per environment (Dev, Test, Prod) per product team. Each subscription belongs to one Entra ID tenant.
Deployment and lifecycle boundary. All resources for one application go in one RG — deleting the RG deletes everything in it. Resources can span regions but share the same lifecycle.
Individual Azure services. Each inherits RBAC and policy from its RG, subscription, and MG.

Azure Policy evaluates resources against business rules. Policies can audit non-compliant resources, deny non-compliant deployments, or automatically remediate drift via DeployIfNotExists and Modify effects.

# Assign built-in policy: restrict allowed Azure regions
az policy assignment create \
  --name "allowed-locations" \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>" \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope"]}}'

# Assign deny policy: require HTTPS-only on storage accounts
az policy assignment create \
  --name "storage-https-only" \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/404c3081-a854-4457-ae30-26a93ef643f9" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>" \
  --enforcement-mode Default  # 'DoNotEnforce' = audit only; 'Default' = enforce

# Check compliance
az policy state list \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "[?complianceState=='NonCompliant'].{resource:resourceId,policy:policyDefinitionName}" \
  -o table

# Trigger auto-remediation for DeployIfNotExists policies
az policy remediation create \
  --name "fix-storage-https" \
  --policy-assignment "storage-https-only" \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance

RBAC controls who can do what to which Azure resources. Roles are assigned to security principals (users, groups, managed identities) at a scope (management group, subscription, resource group, or resource).

# Grant a team member Contributor on a resource group
az role assignment create \
  --assignee "user@company.com" \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg"

# Grant a managed identity secretless access to storage (no connection string needed)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id "<managed-identity-principal-id>" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa"

# List all role assignments on a subscription
az role assignment list --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>" -o table
Resource locks prevent accidental deletion or modification regardless of RBAC permissions. Even an Owner cannot delete a CanNotDelete-locked resource without first removing the lock.

# Prevent deletion of a production database
az lock create \
  --name "prod-db-lock" \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Sql/servers/databases \
  --resource-name prod-db \
  --parent prod-sql-server

# ReadOnly lock: prevents modification AND deletion
az lock create \
  --name "core-vnet-readonly" \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --resource-group network-rg \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks \
  --resource-name prod-vnet
Manageability in cloud means the ability to deploy, monitor, and operate resources consistently; the tooling to automate operations; and the observability to detect and respond to issues.
Web UI at portal.azure.com. Suitable for exploration and one-off tasks. Not suitable for repeatable deployments — click-ops does not version-control.
prefix. Scriptable and available in shell scripts, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps pipelines.
Az PowerShell module. Preferred in Windows environments and for Entra ID scripting.
Declarative infrastructure as code. Bicep compiles to ARM JSON. Both are idempotent — re-running the same template produces the same infrastructure state.
Everything in Azure is an ARM REST API call. SDKs for Python, .NET, Java, Go, and JavaScript wrap these calls with typed objects.

Azure Monitor collects metrics (numerical time-series), logs (structured records in Log Analytics workspace), and traces (distributed request flows from Application Insights). All three feed into alerts, dashboards, and automated responses.

# Create a metric alert: notify when VM CPU exceeds 90% for 5 minutes
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name "vm-cpu-critical" \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/web-vm-01" \
  --condition "avg Percentage CPU > 90" \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --severity 1 \
  --action "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team"

# Log Analytics KQL: find all failed HTTP requests in the last hour
# Run in your Log Analytics workspace
#
# AppRequests
# | where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
# | where Success == false
# | summarize count() by ResultCode, Name
# | order by count_ desc
#
# KQL (Kusto Query Language) is used across Azure Monitor, Sentinel, and Defender

Azure Advisor is a free recommendation engine that analyzes your Azure usage and configurations and provides personalized recommendations across five categories: Reliability, Security, Performance, Cost, and Operational Excellence.

# List Advisor recommendations
az advisor recommendation list \
  --subscription <sub> \
  --query "[].{category:category,impact:impact,problem:shortDescription.problem}" \
  -o table

# Example recommendations:
# Cost:        '3 underutilized VMs - right-size to save $240/month'
# Security:    'Enable MFA for 5 admin accounts'
# Reliability: 'Enable zone redundancy for Azure SQL in prod-rg'
# Performance: 'Enable Read Replicas for this Cosmos DB account'
Infrastructure as Code for Consistent Deployments
Manageability includes deploying infrastructure consistently and reproducibly. Bicep templates encode governance requirements at deployment time, preventing configuration drift between environments.
How the Three Pillars Work Together
Azure Policy sets guardrails (no public storage, Defender must be enabled, allowed locations only).
Defender for Cloud audits compliance; Entra ID enforces MFA; Key Vault secures all secrets from those governed resources.
Azure Monitor alerts when drift occurs; Automation runbooks remediate automatically; Bicep prevents drift at deployment time.
Surfaces improvements the automated systems detected but did not auto-remediate — human-in-the-loop review of the combined posture.
Treating shared responsibility as 'Azure handles security.'

The provider handles infrastructure layers. Misconfigured storage accounts, missing MFA, and SQL servers open to 0.0.0.0/0 are customer-layer failures. The majority of cloud security incidents are misconfiguration, not provider breaches.
Skipping Azure Policy until something goes wrong.
Apply policies early in audit mode first, then switch to enforce mode once the baseline is clean. Retroactive policy enforcement on non-compliant resources can cause deployment failures.
Storing secrets in code or environment variables.
Every secret in code is a breach waiting to happen. Store every secret in Key Vault and retrieve at runtime via managed identity.
Not setting resource locks on production databases.
A CanNotDelete lock on the database is the last line of defense before accidental data loss from an errant az group delete command.

Cloud security is not a one-time configuration — it requires continuous operations. Azure provides an integrated security operations model that combines detection, investigation, and response across all workloads.
Microsoft Sentinel: Cloud-Native SIEM and SOAR

Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platform. Unlike traditional SIEMs that require on-premises hardware, Sentinel scales automatically with your data volume and charges only for what you ingest.

# Enable Microsoft Sentinel on a Log Analytics workspace
RG="security-rg"
WORKSPACE="sentinel-workspace"

az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --workspace-name $WORKSPACE \
  --location eastus

# Enable Sentinel on the workspace
az sentinel workspace create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --workspace-name $WORKSPACE

# Connect Azure Active Directory data connector
az sentinel data-connector create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --workspace-name $WORKSPACE \
  --data-connector-id AzureActiveDirectory \
  --kind AzureActiveDirectory

# Create an analytics rule to detect suspicious sign-ins
az sentinel alert-rule create \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --workspace-name $WORKSPACE \
  --rule-name "Suspicious Sign-In Locations" \
  --kind Scheduled \
  --display-name "Users signing in from multiple geographies" \
  --query "SigninLogs | where ResultType == 0 | summarize Locations=make_set(Location) by UserPrincipalName | where array_length(Locations) > 2" \
  --query-frequency PT1H \
  --query-period PT1H \
  --severity Medium \
  --enabled true

Sentinel organizes security events into incidents, automatically correlating related alerts into a single investigation object. The following KQL query identifies high-severity incidents and their timeline:

// Query all high/critical incidents in the last 7 days with entity details
SecurityIncident
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where Severity in ("High", "Critical")
| extend Entities = parse_json(tostring(Entities))
| mv-expand Entity = Entities
| extend EntityKind = tostring(Entity.kind)
| extend EntityName = coalesce(
    tostring(Entity.properties.userPrincipalName),
    tostring(Entity.properties.address),
    tostring(Entity.properties.friendlyName)
  )
| summarize
    IncidentCount = dcount(IncidentNumber),
    EntityList = make_set(EntityName)
  by Status, Severity
| order by Severity asc

Governance extends beyond security to cost management. Azure Cost Management + Billing provides visibility, allocation, and optimization tools so teams can apply FinOps principles — treating cloud spend as a shared engineering responsibility.

# Create a monthly budget with email alerts at 80% and 100%
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "prod-monthly-budget" \
  --amount 10000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --time-period-start 2025-01-01 \
  --time-period-end 2026-01-01 \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --notifications '[{
    "enabled": true,
    "operator": "GreaterThan",
    "threshold": 80,
    "contactEmails": ["finops@company.com"],
    "thresholdType": "Actual"
  }, {
    "enabled": true,
    "operator": "GreaterThan",
    "threshold": 100,
    "contactEmails": ["finops@company.com", "cto@company.com"],
    "thresholdType": "Actual"
  }]'

# Export cost data to a storage account for custom analysis
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "monthly-cost-export" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --storage-account-id /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/exports-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/costexports \
  --recurrence Monthly \
  --recurrence-period from=2025-01-01 to=2026-01-01 \
  --schedule-status Active
Tags are the foundation of cost allocation. An Azure Policy can enforce that all resource groups carry required cost-allocation tags:

# Assign a built-in policy requiring cost-allocation tags
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-cost-tags" \
  --display-name "Require CostCenter and Project tags" \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --params '{
    "tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"},
    "tagName2": {"value": "Project"}
  }'

# Query resource costs grouped by tag using Resource Graph
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups'
| where isnotempty(tags.CostCenter)
| project name, CostCenter=tags.CostCenter, Project=tags.Project
| order by CostCenter asc
"

Organizations operating under regulatory frameworks (HIPAA, PCI DSS, ISO 27001, SOC 2, FedRAMP) can use Azure's compliance tooling to continuously demonstrate adherence rather than preparing evidence only at audit time.
Encryption at rest/in transit, audit logs, access controls
Network segmentation, cardholder data isolation, WAF
Risk assessment, asset management, incident management

Compliance Manager in the Microsoft Purview portal maps your Azure configuration to regulatory controls and assigns an improvement score. Each "improvement action" links to the specific Azure setting, policy, or documentation needed to meet that control:
: Percentage of controls satisfied, weighted by control importance.
: Groups controls by domain (access, data protection, incident response).
: Attach audit artifacts directly to each control for auditor review.
: A single control can satisfy requirements across multiple frameworks simultaneously.
Putting It All Together: A Governance Reference Architecture
A mature Azure governance architecture layers all three pillars — security, governance, and manageability — from the management group root down to individual resources:
: Assign Azure Security Benchmark initiative, require diagnostic settings for all resource types, deny public IP addresses where not needed.
: Host shared services (Log Analytics workspace, Sentinel, Defender for Cloud, Key Vault, Azure Monitor action groups). Central security team owns these.
: Application teams deploy workloads here. Policy inheritance from root + platform management groups enforces guardrails automatically.
: Required tags (CostCenter, Environment, Owner) enforced by policy. Resource locks on production groups prevent accidental deletion.
: RBAC roles assigned at minimum necessary scope. Key Vault used for all secrets. Private Endpoints eliminate public exposure.
Automation with Azure Automation and Update Management

Manageability at scale requires automating routine operations. Azure Automation provides runbooks (PowerShell or Python) for scheduled tasks, and Update Management ensures OS patches are applied consistently across thousands of VMs:

# Create an Automation Account and enable Update Management
$rg = "automation-rg"
$automationAccount = "corp-automation"
$workspaceId = "/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/security-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/sentinel-workspace"

# Create the Automation Account
New-AzAutomationAccount `
  -ResourceGroupName $rg `
  -Name $automationAccount `
  -Location "EastUS" `
  -AssignSystemIdentity

# Link to Log Analytics for Update Management
Set-AzOperationalInsightsIntelligencePack `
  -ResourceGroupName $rg `
  -WorkspaceName "sentinel-workspace" `
  -IntelligencePackName "Updates" `
  -Enabled $true

# Create a software update configuration targeting production VMs
New-AzAutomationSoftwareUpdateConfiguration `
  -ResourceGroupName $rg `
  -AutomationAccountName $automationAccount `
  -Schedule (New-AzAutomationSchedule `
      -ResourceGroupName $rg `
      -AutomationAccountName $automationAccount `
      -Name "WeeklyCriticalUpdates" `
      -StartTime (Get-Date).AddDays(1) `
      -WeekInterval 1 `
      -DaysOfWeek Saturday) `
  -Windows `
  -IncludedUpdateClassification Critical, Security `
  -AzureVirtualMachineScope @("/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/prod-rg")

Security: built on the shared responsibility model (provider secures physical and hypervisor layers; customer secures OS, identity, and data). Zero Trust (verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach) replaces the network perimeter model.
Defender for Cloud provides unified security posture and threat detection; Entra ID with Conditional Access and PIM is the identity perimeter; Key Vault eliminates secrets from code.

Governance uses the MG to Subscription to RG to Resource hierarchy for policy inheritance. Azure Policy enforces business rules automatically across the fleet — audit, deny, or remediate non-compliant resources at scale.
Azure RBAC implements least privilege. Managed identities (for services) and PIM (for humans) minimize credential exposure.

Manageability covers deployment (Bicep/ARM), monitoring (Azure Monitor + Log Analytics + Application Insights + Advisor), and operations (Automation, Update Manager). Infrastructure as Code prevents configuration drift.
All three pillars form a continuous loop: governance sets rules, security enforces and monitors, manageability makes the posture visible and operable.
